name: Artificial Intelligence
description: The simulation of human intelligence by machines, encompassing learning, reasoning, problem-solving, perception, and language understanding. The broadest field in computer science aiming to create intelligent systems.
overview: Artificial Intelligence is the broad discipline of creating intelligent machines that can perceive, reason, learn, and act. Founded at the Dartmouth Conference in 1956, AI has undergone several waves: symbolic AI (expert systems, logic), statistical learning (ML), and the current deep learning revolution powered by large neural networks and massive data. AI encompasses subfields including machine learning, natural language processing, computer vision, robotics, knowledge representation, automated reasoning, and planning. Modern AI achievements include GPT-4, DALL-E, AlphaFold, self-driving cars, and game-playing systems (AlphaGo, AlphaZero). AI raises profound technical challenges (generalization, interpretability, alignment) and societal questions (ethics, fairness, job displacement). The field is advancing rapidly with transformer architectures, foundation models, and multimodal AI systems.

learningPath.title: AI Foundations & Mathematics
learningPath.description: Build the mathematical and conceptual foundations needed for AI: linear algebra, calculus, probability, statistics, and AI history.
topics: History of AI: Dartmouth Conference, AI winters and springs
What is intelligence? Turing Test, Chinese Room, definitions
Types of AI: Narrow (weak) vs General (strong) vs Superintelligence
AI subfields overview: ML, NLP, CV, robotics, expert systems
Linear algebra for AI: vectors, matrices, eigenvalues, SVD
Calculus: partial derivatives, gradients, chain rule, Jacobians
Probability: axioms, Bayes' theorem, distributions, expectations
Statistics: descriptive, inferential, hypothesis testing, correlation
Information theory: entropy, cross-entropy, KL divergence, mutual information
Optimization: gradient descent, convex optimization, Lagrange multipliers
Complexity theory basics for AI algorithms
Graph theory: search spaces, trees, graphs, heuristics

learningPath.title: Problem Solving & Search Algorithms
learningPath.description: Learn classical AI problem-solving techniques: search algorithms, constraint satisfaction, and game playing.
topics: Problem formulation: state space, initial state, goal test, actions
Uninformed search: BFS, DFS, depth-limited, iterative deepening, uniform cost
Informed search: Greedy best-first, A* algorithm, admissible heuristics
Heuristic functions: design, properties (admissibility, consistency)
Local search: hill climbing, simulated annealing, genetic algorithms
Constraint Satisfaction Problems (CSP): backtracking, forward checking, arc consistency
Game playing: minimax algorithm, alpha-beta pruning
Adversarial search: expectiminimax for stochastic games
Knowledge representation: propositional logic, first-order logic
Resolution and unification in logic
Reasoning under uncertainty: Bayesian networks, Markov models
Planning: STRIPS, PDDL, hierarchical task networks

learningPath.title: Probability, Uncertainty & Bayesian Methods
learningPath.description: Master probabilistic reasoning, Bayesian inference, and handling uncertainty in AI systems.
topics: Bayesian inference: prior, likelihood, posterior, evidence
Probabilistic graphical models: Bayesian networks, Markov random fields
Inference in Bayesian networks: exact (variable elimination), approximate (sampling)
Hidden Markov Models (HMM): forward-backward, Viterbi, Baum-Welch
Markov decision processes (MDP): states, actions, rewards, transition model
Kalman filters for state estimation
Particle filters for robot localization
Naive Bayes classifier — probabilistic ML foundational model
Monte Carlo methods: rejection sampling, importance sampling, MCMC
Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) and Expectation-Maximization (EM)
Causality and counterfactual reasoning (Pearl)

learningPath.title: Machine Learning Fundamentals
learningPath.description: Core machine learning algorithms: supervised, unsupervised, and ensemble methods with practical implementation.
topics: Supervised learning: regression (linear, polynomial, ridge, lasso)
Supervised learning: classification (logistic regression, decision trees, SVM)
k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN): distance metrics, curse of dimensionality
Decision trees: ID3, C4.5, CART, pruning, feature importance
Ensemble methods: bagging, random forests, boosting (AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost)
Support Vector Machines: kernels (linear, RBF, polynomial), soft margin
Unsupervised learning: k-means clustering, DBSCAN, hierarchical clustering
Dimensionality reduction: PCA, t-SNE, UMAP
Model evaluation: cross-validation, bias-variance tradeoff
Metrics: accuracy, precision, recall, F1, ROC-AUC, confusion matrix
Feature engineering and feature selection
Regularization: L1 (Lasso), L2 (Ridge), ElasticNet
Hyperparameter tuning: grid search, random search, Bayesian optimization
Imbalanced datasets: SMOTE, class weights, sampling strategies

learningPath.title: Deep Learning & Neural Networks
learningPath.description: Deep dive into neural networks: architectures, training, regularization, and modern deep learning techniques.
topics: Perceptron and multilayer perceptrons (MLP)
Activation functions: sigmoid, tanh, ReLU, LeakyReLU, GELU, Swish
Backpropagation: chain rule, gradient computation, computational graphs
Optimization: SGD, Momentum, NAG, AdaGrad, RMSprop, Adam, AdamW
Weight initialization: Xavier/Glorot, He initialization
Regularization: Dropout, BatchNorm, LayerNorm, weight decay, data augmentation
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN): convolution, pooling, padding, stride
CNN architectures: LeNet, AlexNet, VGG, ResNet, Inception, EfficientNet
Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN): vanilla, LSTM, GRU, bidirectional
Attention mechanism: Bahdanau, Luong, self-attention
Transformers: multi-head attention, positional encoding, feed-forward
Transformer architectures: BERT, GPT, T5, ViT
Transfer learning and fine-tuning
Autoencoders: vanilla, denoising, variational (VAE)
Generative models: GANs (DCGAN, StyleGAN, Pix2Pix)
Diffusion models: DDPM, latent diffusion, Stable Diffusion
Graph Neural Networks (GNN): GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE
Neural Architecture Search (NAS)

learningPath.title: Natural Language Processing (NLP)
learningPath.description: Master NLP techniques from tokenization to modern large language models and transformers.
topics: Text preprocessing: tokenization, stemming, lemmatization, stop words
Text representation: Bag-of-Words, TF-IDF, N-grams
Word embeddings: Word2Vec (CBOW, Skip-gram), GloVe, FastText
Sequence models: BiLSTM, GRU, encoder-decoder architecture
Attention and transformers for NLP
BERT: masked language modeling, next sentence prediction, fine-tuning
GPT: autoregressive language modeling, prompt engineering, in-context learning
Sequence-to-sequence models: T5, BART, mT5
Named Entity Recognition (NER)
Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging and syntactic parsing
Sentiment analysis and emotion detection
Machine translation: encoder-decoder with attention
Text summarization: extractive vs abstractive
Question answering: extractive, generative, retrieval-augmented (RAG)
Topic modeling: LDA, NMF, BERTopic
Large Language Models (LLMs): scaling laws, RLHF, instruction tuning
Prompt engineering: few-shot, chain-of-thought, tree-of-thought
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG): indexing, retrieval, generation
LLM fine-tuning: LoRA, QLoRA, PEFT techniques

learningPath.title: Computer Vision
learningPath.description: Learn computer vision fundamentals: image processing, object detection, segmentation, and generative vision models.
topics: Image processing: filters, edge detection (Sobel, Canny), Hough transform
Feature extraction: SIFT, SURF, ORB, HOG
Image classification: CNN architectures, ImageNet, fine-tuning
Object detection: R-CNN, Fast R-CNN, Faster R-CNN, YOLO, SSD
Instance segmentation: Mask R-CNN, YOLACT
Semantic segmentation: U-Net, DeepLab, FCN
Object tracking: SORT, DeepSORT, Kalman filters
Optical flow: Lucas-Kanade, Farneback, RAFT
Image generation: GANs, diffusion models, DALL-E, Stable Diffusion
3D vision: stereo vision, structure from motion, NeRF
Video understanding: action recognition, video captioning
Vision Transformers (ViT): patches, positional embeddings, classification
Multimodal models: CLIP, BLIP, Flamingo
Face recognition: FaceNet, ArcFace, DeepFace
Medical image analysis: segmentation, classification, detection
Autonomous driving perception: lane detection, obstacle detection

learningPath.title: Reinforcement Learning
learningPath.description: Learn RL from basic bandits to deep RL: value-based, policy-based, model-based methods, and multi-agent systems.
topics: Markov Decision Processes (MDP): formal definition, Bellman equations
Dynamic programming: policy iteration, value iteration
Monte Carlo methods: first-visit, every-visit, exploring starts
Temporal Difference (TD) learning: TD(0), SARSA, Q-learning
Eligibility traces: TD(λ), SARSA(λ)
Function approximation in RL: linear, neural network
Deep Q-Networks (DQN): experience replay, target network
DQN improvements: Double DQN, Dueling DQN, Prioritized Replay
Policy gradient methods: REINFORCE, actor-critic
Advanced policy gradients: PPO, TRPO, A2C, A3C
Deterministic policy gradients: DDPG, TD3, SAC
Model-based RL: Dyna-Q, MuZero, Dreamer
Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) and Imitation Learning
Exploration vs exploitation: UCB, Thompson sampling, intrinsic motivation
Multi-agent RL: independent, centralized, mixed approaches
RL for robotics: sim-to-real, domain randomization
RL for games: AlphaGo, AlphaZero, OpenAI Five

learningPath.title: AI Ethics, Safety & Alignment
learningPath.description: Understand AI ethics, bias, fairness, interpretability, safety, and alignment with human values.
topics: AI ethics frameworks: fairness, accountability, transparency, explainability
Bias in AI: data bias, algorithmic bias, measurement bias, representation bias
Fairness metrics: demographic parity, equal opportunity, equalized odds
Explainable AI (XAI): LIME, SHAP, Grad-CAM, integrated gradients
Model interpretability: intrinsic vs post-hoc, global vs local
Privacy: differential privacy, federated learning, on-device ML
Robustness: adversarial attacks and defenses (FGSM, PGD, adversarial training)
AI safety: specification gaming, reward hacking, distributional shift
Alignment problem: outer alignment, inner alignment, goal misgeneralization
AI governance: regulations (EU AI Act), auditing, certification
Risks from advanced AI: misuse, accidents, existential risk
Responsible AI development: red teaming, evaluation, monitoring
Environmental impact of AI: compute cost, energy, carbon footprint
AI for social good: healthcare, climate, education, accessibility
projects: Bias audit of ML model with fairness metrics

learningPath.title: Advanced AI Topics & Research
learningPath.description: Explore cutting-edge AI: multi-modal models, foundation models, reasoning, planning, and AI agent systems.
topics: Foundation models: GPT-4, Claude, Gemini, Llama — architecture and capabilities
Multi-modal AI: vision-language models, speech-text integration
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG): advanced indexing, hybrid search, re-ranking
AI agents: tool use, planning, memory, reflection (ReAct, AutoGPT, LangChain)
Chain-of-thought reasoning: zero-shot, few-shot, tree-of-thought, graph-of-thought
Constitutional AI and RLHF for alignment
Mixture of Experts (MoE) architecture
Long-context transformers: RoPE scaling, RingAttention, Infini-Attention
Efficient AI: quantization (GPTQ, AWQ), pruning, distillation, FlashAttention
AI for science: AlphaFold, drug discovery, weather prediction
Edge AI: on-device inference, TinyML, mobile models
Neural-symbolic AI: combining neural networks with symbolic reasoning
Causal AI: causal discovery, causal inference, do-calculus
Evolutionary and neuroevolution approaches
Quantum machine learning: quantum neural networks, variational circuits
Neuromorphic computing: spiking neural networks, event-based computation
projects: AI agent with tool use and planning

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between AI, ML, and Deep Learning?
interviewQuestions.answer: AI is the broad field of creating intelligent machines. ML is a subset of AI where systems learn from data without explicit programming. Deep Learning is a subset of ML using multi-layered neural networks to learn hierarchical representations. All DL is ML, all ML is AI, but not vice versa. Example: rule-based chess AI is not ML; linear regression is ML; CNNs for image classification is DL.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the A* search algorithm and its optimality.
interviewQuestions.answer: A* combines the cost to reach a node (g) and a heuristic estimate to the goal (h). It maintains an open set prioritized by f = g + h. A* is optimal if the heuristic is admissible (never overestimates) and consistent (h(n) <= c(n,a,n') + h(n')). A* is complete if branching factor is finite. Dijkstra is A* with h=0.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning?
interviewQuestions.answer: Supervised: learns from labeled data (input, output pairs), goal is to map inputs to outputs. Unsupervised: discovers hidden patterns in unlabeled data (clustering, dimensionality reduction). Reinforcement: learns through interaction by maximizing cumulative reward, no labels, learns from consequences of actions.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the attention mechanism and why is it important?
interviewQuestions.answer: Attention allows models to focus on relevant parts of the input when producing output. It computes weighted sums of values based on query-key similarity. Self-attention (transformers) allows each position to attend to all positions, capturing long-range dependencies without recurrence. Multi-head attention runs multiple attention operations in parallel. Attention enabled LLMs by solving the long-range dependency bottleneck of RNNs.

interviewQuestions.question: What is overfitting and how do you prevent it?
interviewQuestions.answer: Overfitting is when a model learns training data too well, including noise, and fails to generalize to new data. Signs: high training accuracy but poor validation accuracy. Prevention: more training data, cross-validation, regularization (L1, L2), dropout (neural networks), early stopping, reducing model complexity, feature selection, data augmentation, ensemble methods.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the bias-variance tradeoff.
interviewQuestions.answer: Bias is error from overly simplistic models (underfitting) — wrong assumptions about the data. Variance is error from overly complex models (overfitting) — sensitive to training data fluctuations. High bias: models miss relevant patterns. High variance: models learn noise. The tradeoff: increasing complexity reduces bias but increases variance. Optimal model balances both for minimum total error.

interviewQuestions.question: What are generative vs discriminative models?
interviewQuestions.answer: Generative models learn P(X, Y) — joint distribution of inputs and labels — and can generate new data. Examples: Naive Bayes, GANs, VAEs, diffusion models. Discriminative models learn P(Y|X) — conditional probability of label given input — focusing on decision boundary. Examples: logistic regression, SVM, neural networks. Generative models can create data; discriminative models typically have higher classification accuracy.

books.title: Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach
books.author: Stuart Russell & Peter Norvig
books.description: The definitive AI textbook covering everything from search and logic to ML, NLP, robotics, and AI ethics. Used at top universities worldwide.

books.title: Deep Learning
books.author: Ian Goodfellow, Yoshua Bengio, Aaron Courville
books.description: The MIT Press deep learning textbook covering mathematical foundations, neural network architectures, and advanced topics.

books.title: Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction
books.author: Richard Sutton & Andrew Barto
books.description: The classic RL textbook covering bandits, DP, TD learning, policy gradients, and advanced RL algorithms.

books.title: Speech and Language Processing
books.author: Dan Jurafsky & James H. Martin
books.description: Comprehensive NLP textbook covering text processing, syntax, semantics, dialogue, and modern LLMs.

books.title: Computer Vision: Algorithms and Applications
books.author: Richard Szeliski
books.description: Comprehensive CV textbook covering image formation, feature detection, segmentation, object recognition, and 3D vision.

books.title: The Quest for Artificial Intelligence
books.author: Nils J. Nilsson
books.description: A fascinating history of AI from its origins to modern systems, covering key developments and researchers.

bestPractices: Start simple — use a simple baseline (linear model, heuristic) before complex deep learning
Always split data into train/validation/test sets — never let test data influence model decisions
Use cross-validation when working with small datasets
Normalize/standardize features for gradient-based learning methods
Use appropriate evaluation metrics — accuracy is misleading for imbalanced datasets
Monitor for data leakage — ensure no information from the future/test set leaks into training
Version control datasets, models, and experiments (DVC, MLflow, Weights & Biases)
Use reproducible random seeds for experiments
Log all experiments: hyperparameters, metrics, model artifacts
Document assumptions, data sources, preprocessing steps, and model limitations
Start with interpretable models when possible — explainability is crucial for production
Test on edge cases and distribution shifts before deployment
Implement monitoring for model drift and data drift in production
Follow ethical AI guidelines: fairness audits, bias testing, privacy protection
Use appropriate hardware — start with CPU/small models, scale to GPU/TPU as needed
Keep up with research — the field advances rapidly, follow arXiv, Twitter, conferences
Prefer simple solutions: if a rule-based system works, don't build a neural network
Always baseline against random/naive predictions to validate your model adds value

careerOpportunities.description: Conduct original research to advance AI capabilities, publish papers at top conferences, and develop novel algorithms.
Build and deploy AI systems, implement ML models, optimize inference, and integrate AI into products.
Build natural language processing systems including chatbots, information extraction, translation, and text analytics.
Develop visual recognition systems for object detection, segmentation, face recognition, and video analysis.
Design RL agents for robotics, games, recommendation systems, and autonomous decision-making.
Research how to ensure AI systems are safe, aligned with human values, robust, and interpretable.
